probes.auth: none — fully open, no key/signup required
probes.response_shape: Top-level: {result: {input: {...}, addressMatches: [...]}}. Each element of addressMatches has: matchedAddress (string, "601 UNIVERSITY DR, SAN MARCOS, TX, 78666"), coordinates {x,y}, tigerLine {tigerLineId, side}, and geographies (object keyed by layer name). Layer keys observed for this address: States, County Subdivisions, Urban Areas, Incorporated Places, Counties, "2024 State Legislative Districts - Upper", "2024 State Legislative Districts - Lower", "2020 Census Blocks", Census Tracts, "119th Congressional Districts". Each layer is an array of one object with fields like GEOID, NAME, BASENAME, STATE, plus layer-specific fields (CD119, SLDU, SLDL, COUNTY, TRACT, BLOCK).
probes.rate_limits: No documented hard rate limit for this endpoint; Census recommends the free service for light/interactive use and their bulk batch endpoint or TIGERweb for heavy volume. No observed 429 in this probe (1 request made). Treat as best-effort/unthrottled for hackathon-scale traffic but don't hammer it in a loop.
probes.app_value: This is the address-to-district resolver: for one free, keyless GET call the app gets the voter's congressional district (119th Congress, e.g. TX-35, GEOID 4835), state senate/house districts (2024 SLDU/SLDL, e.g. TX Senate 21, House 45), and county (Hays County, GEOID 48209) — everything needed as the first hop to then look up races/candidates for the Nov 2026 midterms.
probes.notes: Verified live via actual curl (not fabricated): HTTP 200, address "601 University Dr, San Marcos, TX 78666" matched exactly, and the "119th Congressional Districts" layer confirms this is the current Congress vintage as required. State legislative districts returned are labeled "2024 State Legislative Districts" (current TX district lines) — did not separately verify these reflect any post-2024 redistricting changes, but they are the Census's "Current" vintage so should be authoritative as of today. Only tested one address/one benchmark-vintage combo (Public_AR_Current / Current_Current); did not test malformed addresses, non-matching addresses, or the batch/bulk endpoint. GEOIDs returned (state+county+cousub+tract+block, CD119, SLDU/SLDL) are standard Census geography codes suitable as join keys into other Census/BLS/OpenFEC-adjacent datasets if needed later.

probes.auth: none — fully public ArcGIS REST service, no key/token required
probes.sample_request: curl 'https://tigerweb.geo.census.gov/arcgis/rest/services/TIGERweb/Legislative/MapServer/4/query?geometry=-97.9384,29.8884&geometryType=esriGeometryPoint&inSR=4326&spatialRel=esriSpatialRelIntersects&outFields=*&returnGeometry=false&f=json'  (layer 4 = "119th Congressional Districts"; layer 6 = "2024 State Legislative Districts - Lower" queried the same way)
probes.response_shape: Standard Esri ArcGIS REST query response: top-level {displayFieldName, fieldAliases, fields[], features[]}. Each feature has an "attributes" object. Most useful fields: NAME ("Congressional District 35" / "State House District 45"), GEOID (4-digit for CD: state FIPS+district, e.g. "4835"; 5-digit for state lower/upper), BASENAME (bare district number, "35"/"45"), STATE (2-digit FIPS, "48"=TX), CD119/CDSESSN (Congress session number), CDTYP, AREALAND/AREAWATER, INTPTLAT/INTPTLON (internal point coords), LSADC/MTFCC (Census classification codes), OBJECTID.
probes.rate_limits: Not documented/observed in this probe; public Esri-hosted Census service, no key means no per-key quota — typical shared-server soft throttling only. Not stress-tested here.
probes.app_value: Gives free, keyless point-in-polygon lookup of a voter's current (119th Congress) congressional district and state upper/lower legislative districts directly from lon/lat — usable as the ballot-district resolver, or as a cross-check/fallback if the primary geocoder's district data is stale or missing. Also exposes district boundary polygons (via returnGeometry=true) for map display.
probes.notes: Verified with 3 live requests, all HTTP 200, real non-fabricated data: (1) MapServer root f=pjson listed layers including id 0/4 "119th Congressional Districts" and id 1/2/5/6 "2024 State Legislative Districts - Upper/Lower"; (2) point query on layer 4 for San Marcos, TX (-97.9384, 29.8884) returned Congressional District 35, GEOID 4835, STATE 48; (3) point query on layer 6 (State House/Lower) returned "State House District 45", GEOID 48045. Did not test the "identify" endpoint variant specifically (used "query" instead, which is the more standard/reliable path and worked on first try) — not needed since query succeeded. Did not test Senate (upper, layer 5) but same pattern should work. Did not load WebFetch/docs since keyless success was immediate. Boundaries reflect current 119th Congress / 2024 state district vintage, appropriate for Nov 2026 midterms. No rate-limit stress test performed — treat with normal courtesy (cache results, don't hammer per-keystroke).

probes.auth: none — fully public, no key, no rate-limit headers observed
probes.sample_request: curl -sS -m 20 'https://clerk.house.gov/evs/2026/roll030.xml'  (also verified 'https://clerk.house.gov/evs/2025/roll050.xml') — both returned HTTP 200 with full well-formed XML
probes.response_shape:
Root element <rollcall-vote> with two children:
1. <vote-metadata>: congress (e.g. 119), session (1st/2nd), chamber, rollcall-num, legis-num (bill id e.g. "H R 2988"), vote-question (e.g. "On Motion to Recommit", "On Agreeing to the Resolution, as Amended"), vote-type (YEA-AND-NAY), vote-result (Passed/Failed), action-date, action-time, vote-desc (plain-English bill description), vote-totals (party-by-party yea/nay/present/not-voting breakdown, R/D/I).
2. <vote-data>: repeated <recorded-vote> elements, each containing <legislator name-id="A000370" sort-field="Adams" unaccented-name="Adams" party="D" state="NC" role="legislator">Adams</legislator><vote>Yea|Nay|Present|Not Voting</vote></recorded-vote>.
No JSON alternative observed; must parse XML (DTD referenced but not required for parsing).
probes.rate_limits: None observed/documented in this probe — no headers indicating throttling; this is a static file server (each roll call is its own XML file), so effectively unlimited for read access. No official rate-limit docs found (not checked further per scope — plain static XML, no API gateway).
probes.app_value: Gives real, per-member, per-bill House roll-call votes (bill, question, date, result, and each incumbent's Yea/Nay/Present/Not Voting) that can be joined to a voter's district representative via name-id/state/party to build sourced, cited "here's how your incumbent actually voted" insights — a strong, verifiable input for the personalized-insights feature.
probes.notes: Caveats: (1) URLs are per-roll-call and must be enumerated sequentially (roll001.xml, roll002.xml, ...) per year — there is no bulk/list/index JSON endpoint verified in this probe; you'd need to either scrape https://clerk.house.gov/Votes for an index or increment roll numbers and handle 404s to discover the current max for a session. (2) legislator name-id (e.g. "A000370") is the Bioguide ID, which is the standard key to join against other congress data sources (e.g. ProPublica/Congress.gov/unitedstates/congress-legislators) to get full name, district, bio, etc. — this file alone has only last name + state + party, not district number or first name. (3) Senate uses a different clerk (senate.gov XML, not this endpoint) — this source is House-only. (4) Only tested 2 arbitrary roll numbers; did not verify behavior for an out-of-range roll number (e.g. 404 body) or check for a documented rate limit — low risk for a hackathon given it's static file hosting.

probes.response_shape: Top-level: {description, count, total_pages, next_page_url, results:[...]}. Each result object includes: title, type (e.g. 'Notice','Rule','Proposed Rule'), abstract, document_number, html_url, pdf_url, public_inspection_pdf_url, publication_date, agencies:[{raw_name, name, id, url, json_url, slug}]. Most useful fields for the app: title, abstract, type, agencies[].name, publication_date, html_url (source link for citation), document_number (stable id).
probes.rate_limits: Not observed in this probe (no rate-limit headers checked); Federal Register's public docs state it's free/unauthenticated with generous limits (no key required for reasonable use). Not stress-tested here.
probes.app_value: Gives the voter app recent, citable federal regulatory/agency actions (rules, proposed rules, notices) tied to issues voters care about (e.g., healthcare), each with a direct html_url source link and agency attribution — good for the 'source-linked recent-regulation context' feature without any auth setup.
probes.notes: Verified live and working right now with a single unauthenticated request (HTTP 200). Query for 'healthcare' returned 10,000 total matches across 50 pages (per_page=3), confirming rich, current data (top result dated 2025-12-11). Did not test other endpoints (e.g. /api/v1/articles, agencies endpoint, or filtering by publication_date range/agency) or verify rate-limit headers/pagination behavior beyond next_page_url presence — worth a quick follow-up test at the hackathon if you need date-range filtering (conditions[publication_date][gte]) or agency filtering (conditions[agencies][]) to scope to items relevant to a specific race/issue.

probes.auth: none — fully open, no key/registration needed
probes.response_shape: titles.json: {"titles":[{number, name, latest_amended_on, latest_issue_date, up_to_date_as_of, reserved}, ...]} — one entry per CFR title (50 titles), e.g. Title 11 = "Federal Elections". structure/{date}/title-{n}.json: a recursive tree {identifier, label, label_level, label_description, reserved, type ("title"|"chapter"|"part"|"section"), size, volumes, received_on, children:[...]} down to individual regulation sections (e.g. Title 11 Part 1 = FEC Privacy Act, § 1.1 "Purpose and scope.", etc.). No candidate/race/finance data at all — this is pure CFR regulatory text metadata, not FEC filings or election administration data.
probes.rate_limits: Not documented/observed in this probe; no rate-limit headers surfaced in the truncated output. No auth means presumably some public rate limit exists but wasn't hit in 3 quick requests.
probes.app_value: Very low direct value for this voter app. It only exposes structure/text of the Code of Federal Regulations (e.g., Title 11 = Federal Election Commission regulations, Title 2 = Federal Financial Assistance) — useful only as a citation source if the app wants to link to the actual regulatory text underlying FEC rules/campaign-finance procedures, not for candidate, ballot, or race data. Skip for MVP; not worth engineering time.
probes.notes: Confirmed alive and keyless via 2 live 200 responses today (2026-07-03). Only tested titles.json and structure/{date}/title-11.json; did not test full-text search, ancestry, or corrections endpoints of the versioner API — those exist per eCFR docs but weren't probed here. No candidate/legislator/ballot data present in this API; would need FEC.gov's separate api.open.fec.gov (different source) for campaign finance. Given "low priority" instruction, did not dig further into rate limits or other versioner endpoints.

probes.auth: none — fully open, no API key or registration required for any endpoint tested
probes.sample_request:
curl -sS -X POST 'https://api.usaspending.gov/api/v2/search/spending_by_geography/' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"scope":"place_of_performance","geo_layer":"district","geo_layer_filters":["4831"],"filters":{"time_period":[{"start_date":"2025-10-01","end_date":"2026-07-03"}]}}'
  -> 200 OK: {"scope":"place_of_performance","geo_layer":"district","spending_level":"transactions","results":[{"shape_code":"4831","display_name":"TX-31","aggregated_amount":3642332172.16,"population":801721,"per_capita":4543.14}],"messages":[...]}
Also confirmed: GET https://api.usaspending.gov/api/v2/references/toptier_agencies/ -> 200 OK, list of ~100+ federal agencies with obligated_amount/budget_authority_amount/outlay_amount.
County-level test also succeeded: geo_layer:"county", geo_layer_filters:["48491"] (Williamson County, TX) -> aggregated_amount $3,343,105,740.67, population 609017, per_capita $5489.35.
probes.response_shape:
Top-level JSON object. For spending_by_geography: {scope, geo_layer, spending_level, results:[{shape_code, display_name, aggregated_amount, population, per_capita}], messages:[...]}. results is an array (one element when geo_layer_filters has one code, more if multiple codes/no filter). For toptier_agencies: {results:[{agency_id, toptier_code, abbreviation, agency_name, agency_slug, active_fy, active_fq, outlay_amount, obligated_amount, budget_authority_amount, current_total_budget_authority_amount, percentage_of_total_budget_authority, congressional_justification_url}]}.

Most useful fields for the voter app:
- display_name (e.g. "TX-31" or county name) — human-readable label to show the voter their area
- aggregated_amount — total federal $ obligated/spent in that district/county/state for the period
- per_capita — $ per person, good for a headline stat ("$4,543 per person in federal spending reached your district")
- population
- shape_code — the geo id (state FIPS+district for districts, county FIPS for counties, state FIPS for states) — needed to correlate voter address -> geo code
- geo_layer / scope — confirms which geography level and place_of_performance vs recipient_location
- time_period filter — lets you scope to current FY/appropriations cycle
- agency_name / obligated_amount / budget_authority_amount (from toptier_agencies) — could combine with a follow-up spending_by_award or category breakdown call to attribute district $ to specific agencies/programs (not tested here, but documented at /api/v2/search/spending_by_category/ per the endpoint docs page)
probes.rate_limits: No rate-limit headers observed in these ~4 requests; no documented public rate limit found. USAspending is a public GSA/Treasury-run open data API intended for high-volume use; in practice hackathon-scale traffic is fine. Not verified: any throttling behavior under sustained/bulk load.
probes.app_value: Lets the app show "federal money flowing into your area" cards: total and per-capita federal obligations for the voter's exact congressional district (or county/state), refreshable live at request time with no API key, directly usable as a cited data point next to candidate profiles (e.g., contrasting a district's dependency on federal spending with a candidate's voting record on appropriations).
probes.notes: Requests worked directly via curl — no networking block. One initial POST attempt (state-level TX with place_of_performance_locations filter) timed out after 20s with 0 bytes; likely a slow/heavy query shape for state-wide scope rather than an auth/availability issue, since the district- and county-level POSTs with geo_layer_filters succeeded quickly and reliably. Recommend the app use geo_layer_filters with specific FIPS/district codes (fast path) rather than broad place_of_performance_locations filters (slow path) under a hackathon time budget. District code format required is state-FIPS + 2-digit district number concatenated (e.g. "4831" for TX-31), not "TX-31" or "TX31" as I first guessed — app will need a mapping from (state, CD number) to this code, or from full address via Census geocoder -> FIPS state+CD -> USAspending code. Did not test /docs/endpoints page directly (WebFetch not needed since live requests answered the question), and did not test spending_by_category or award-level endpoints for per-agency-in-district breakdowns — flagged as a follow-up if time allows.

probes.auth: DEMO_KEY works (query param api_key=DEMO_KEY). It's the shared api.data.gov demo key — very low shared rate limit (X-RateLimit-Limit: 10/hr observed), so get a free personal key at https://api.data.gov/signup/ for real hackathon use (instant, email-based, no approval wait) and swap it in.
probes.sample_request: curl -i 'https://api.open.fec.gov/v1/candidates/?api_key=DEMO_KEY&election_year=2026&office=H&state=TX&per_page=3'  ->  HTTP/2 200, 350 TX House candidates found for 2026 cycle. Also verified: curl 'https://api.open.fec.gov/v1/candidate/H6TX31136/totals/?api_key=DEMO_KEY' -> 200 (finance totals) and curl 'https://api.open.fec.gov/v1/candidate/H6TX31136/committees/?api_key=DEMO_KEY' -> 200 (committee linkage).
probes.response_shape: Top-level: {api_version, pagination:{count,pages,page,per_page}, results:[...]}. /candidates/ result fields: candidate_id (e.g. "H6TX31136"), name ("ABEL, WILLIAM P"), party ("REP"), party_full, office ("H"), office_full ("House"), state, district, district_number, candidate_status, incumbent_challenge_full ("Challenger"/"Open seat"/"Incumbent"), has_raised_funds, election_years, cycles, first_file_date. /candidate/{id}/totals/ result fields: candidate_id, cycle, coverage_start_date/coverage_end_date, contributions, individual_contributions, individual_itemized_contributions, disbursements, candidate_contribution, loans, last_cash_on_hand_end_period, last_debts_owed_by_committee, last_report_type_full, last_report_year. /candidate/{id}/committees/ gives committee_id, committee_type_full, city/custodian info — needed to then pull /committee/{id}/schedules/schedule_a/ for itemized donor-level contributions if desired.
probes.rate_limits: Headers observed: x-ratelimit-limit: 10, x-ratelimit-remaining decremented per call (9, then 8, then 8 — two calls in same second may share a bucket tick). This is the shared api.data.gov DEMO_KEY pool (10 req/hour), NOT representative of a personal key's limit (personal keys typically get 1000/hour per api.data.gov standard tier). Get a personal key before the demo.
probes.app_value: This is a solid, ready-to-use campaign-finance backbone: for any candidate resolved from a ballot lookup, look up their FEC candidate_id (match by name/state/office/district), pull /totals/ for headline numbers (total raised, individual vs. large-donor share, cash on hand, self-funding) and /committees/ + schedule_a/schedule_b for itemized donor/spending detail — all directly citable with FEC source links per candidate.
probes.notes: All three endpoint types (candidates search, candidate totals, candidate committees) work right now with DEMO_KEY, no signup needed for the hackathon demo itself, though a personal free key (instant signup at api.data.gov) is strongly recommended to avoid the 10/hr shared-demo ceiling during live testing/dev. Candidate matching to a ballot (by district/state/office) is straightforward since district_number and state are first-class fields. Did not test schedule_a (itemized contributions) or /committee/{id}/filings/ endpoints directly, but they follow the same auth/pagination pattern and are documented at api.open.fec.gov/developers.

probes.auth: api_key=DEMO_KEY works right now for all tested endpoints (200 OK, no 403 seen). No signup needed for hackathon use, though a free instant key is available at https://api.congress.gov/sign-up/ for higher/more reliable rate limits.
probes.response_shape:
Top-level JSON envelope: {<resourceName>: [...], pagination: {count, next}, request: {...}}. Resource arrays are lists of flat objects (not deeply nested) with a `url` field for drill-down.
- /member?limit=N -> {members:[{bioguideId, name, partyName, state, district, depiction.imageUrl, terms.item[{chamber,startYear}], updateDate, url}], pagination}
- /member/congress/{congress}/{state} -> same member shape filtered by state (district also present) - useful for resolving a voter's federal reps by state; note: no ZIP/district-geocoding endpoint, app must map address->state+district itself (e.g. via Census geocoder) then filter/match district here
- /member/{bioguideId}/sponsored-legislation -> {sponsoredLegislation:[{congress, number, type, title, introducedDate, latestAction.{actionDate,text}, policyArea.name, url}]} (cosponsored-legislation endpoint exists analogously per docs, not separately tested)
- /bill?limit=N -> {bills:[{congress, type, number, title, originChamber, latestAction.{actionDate,text}, updateDate, url}]}
- /house-vote?limit=N -> {houseRollCallVotes:[{congress, session, rollCallNumber, identifier, legislationType, legislationNumber, legislationUrl, result, voteType, startDate, sourceDataURL, url}]}; senate-vote endpoint exists analogously per docs, not separately tested here.
Most useful fields for app: bioguideId (join key), name/party/state/district, chamber/startYear (current officeholder), sponsoredLegislation title+latestAction (voting-record/positions narrative), bill title+latestAction (context/citations), roll-call result+legislationUrl (record of how a member voted, though vote-level per-member position requires drilling into the specific roll-call detail endpoint, not fetched in this probe).
probes.rate_limits: Not explicitly observed in headers during this probe (did not capture response headers). Publicly documented DEMO_KEY limit is very low (reportedly ~30-40 req/hour); a free registered key raises this to ~5,000 req/hour. For a hackathon demo with light traffic, DEMO_KEY may suffice but getting a free key (instant, via https://api.congress.gov/sign-up/, email-based) is recommended as a safety margin -- not verified live in this probe since DEMO_KEY never hit a limit in our ~5 requests.
probes.app_value: This is the incumbent-record backbone for federal races (House/Senate): resolve a voter's current member(s) by state/district, pull their sponsored/cosponsored bills for a plain-English "what have they done" narrative, and cite roll-call vote results for specific bills -- all with stable congress.gov URLs to use as citation links.
probes.notes: Confirmed live with real HTTP calls just now (2026-07-03): /member (200), /bill (200), /member/{bioguideId}/sponsored-legislation (200), /member/congress/{congress}/{state} (200), /house-vote (200). Never observed a 403/auth-error from DEMO_KEY in this session, so cannot report verbatim auth-error text -- not needed since it worked. Not tested in this probe (time-capped at 8 requests): /senate-vote, cosponsored-legislation, and per-member roll-call position (how a specific member voted on a specific roll call) -- these are documented sibling endpoints and very likely work the same way but weren't hit live. Congress.gov covers ONLY federal Congress (House reps + Senators) -- no state legislature, governor, or local-race data, so it must be paired with another source (e.g. Ballotpedia/OpenStates/Google Civic) for state/local ballot items. Address-to-district mapping is NOT provided by this API; the app needs a separate geocoding step (e.g. Census Bureau geocoder or Google Civic) to turn a street address into a state+district before querying this API.

probes.auth: DEMO_KEY (api.data.gov shared demo key) works for collections, package summary, and paginated collection listing endpoints with no signup needed. The /search POST endpoint returned HTTP 500 'Oops, Something went wrong, Please contact govinfo team for further assistance' on DEMO_KEY with two different valid-looking JSON bodies (tried with and without a sorts array) — looks like a DEMO_KEY-specific block on /search specifically, not a request-format problem, since other endpoints work fine on DEMO_KEY. A free api.data.gov key (instant signup, no approval wait, at https://api.data.gov/signup/) would very likely fix /search and raise DEMO_KEY's low rate limit.
probes.sample_request: curl 'https://api.govinfo.gov/collections/BILLSTATUS/2026-06-01T00:00:00Z?api_key=DEMO_KEY&pageSize=3&offsetMark=*' -> 200 OK, returns count:4458, nextPage cursor, packages array. Also GET https://api.govinfo.gov/packages/BILLSTATUS-119hr9581/summary?api_key=DEMO_KEY -> 200 OK package metadata. GET https://api.govinfo.gov/collections?api_key=DEMO_KEY -> 200 OK, lists ~40+ collections (BILLS, BILLSTATUS, BILLSUM, CREC, CRPT, PLAW, CFR, ECFR, CHRG, CDIR, USCODE, etc.) with packageCount per collection.
probes.response_shape: Three endpoint families, all flat JSON: (1) GET /collections -> {collections:[{collectionCode, collectionName, packageCount, granuleCount}]} lists available document sets. (2) GET /collections/{COLLECTION}/{startDate}?offsetMark=*&pageSize=N -> {count, nextPage, previousPage, packages:[{packageId, congress, dateIssued, docClass, lastModified, packageLink, title}]} — paginated feed of packages updated since startDate, cursor-based via offsetMark/nextPage. (3) GET /packages/{packageId}/summary -> single package metadata {packageId, congress, billType, billNumber, dateIssued, lastModified, governmentAuthor1/2, download:{xmlLink, pdfLink, txtLink...}, relatedLink, category}. Most useful fields: packageId (e.g. BILLSTATUS-119hr9581), congress, billType/billNumber, dateIssued/lastModified, download.xmlLink (points to the actual BILLSTATUS XML with sponsor, cosponsors, actions, subjects, committees, latestAction — the real bill content lives here, not inline in the summary JSON), title (often null in BILLSTATUS, populated in BILLS/BILLSUM), collectionCode, relatedLink (cross-links bill<->law<->report).
probes.rate_limits: Not directly observed (no 429 in 5 requests); api.data.gov DEMO_KEY is documented elsewhere as a low shared-across-all-users limit (roughly tens of req/hr), too low for live multi-person demo traffic. A free personal api.data.gov key is issued instantly by email with no approval delay and raises the limit substantially — should be obtained before demo day.
probes.app_value: GovInfo is the authoritative bulk source for actual bill text, bill status/actions, sponsors/cosponsors, committee reports, and the Congressional Record — useful as a backup/supplement to Congress.gov for full-text bill content and official metadata, with citable permalinks (packageLink/xmlLink) satisfying the app's 'source-linked' requirement. It is not a candidate/race/campaign-finance database, so it doesn't directly power candidate profiles.
probes.notes: Verified live in this session, DEMO_KEY, 2026-07-03: GET /collections (200), GET /collections/BILLSTATUS/{date}?offsetMark=* (200, needed to add offsetMark=* after an initial 400 asking for it), GET /packages/{id}/summary (200). NOT verified: the /search POST endpoint (HTTP 500 both attempts on DEMO_KEY — could not confirm whether a real api.data.gov key fixes it). Did not fetch an actual bill XML body (download.xmlLink) to inspect the full sponsor/cosponsor/action schema — the package summary only links to it rather than embedding it, so using bill status/text fully requires an XML-parsing step, raising hackathon integration cost versus a source that returns bill status as ready JSON (e.g. Congress.gov API). Recommend: grab a free personal api.data.gov key immediately (instant) to raise the rate limit and retest /search; treat GovInfo as Tier-2/supplementary bulk legislative-document source rather than primary candidate/race data.

probes.auth: Free API key required, obtained by creating an account at https://openstates.org/accounts/signup/ then generating a key at https://open.pluralpolicy.com/accounts/profile/ (this profile URL is where the docs point you after login/registration). Pass the key via `?apikey=KEY` query param or `X-API-KEY` header. No working keyless/demo path exists — every endpoint tested returned 403 without a key. Signup is self-service and should take a few minutes; no indication of manual approval.
probes.sample_request: curl -i 'https://v3.openstates.org/jurisdictions?classification=state&per_page=2' -> HTTP/2 403 with body {"detail":"Must provide API Key as ?apikey or X-API-KEY. Login and visit https://openstates.org/account/profile/ for your API key."}. Also tried curl -i 'https://v3.openstates.org/people.geo?lat=30.5&lng=-97.5' -> identical 403/same error text. Confirmed live API + auth wall, not a dead/broken service. Once a key is obtained, the working call for the app would be: curl -H "X-API-KEY: KEY" 'https://v3.openstates.org/people.geo?lat={lat}&lng={lng}&include=offices'
probes.response_shape:
Pulled the live OpenAPI spec (https://v3.openstates.org/openapi.json, HTTP 200) since Swagger UI is JS-rendered and WebFetch couldn't read it directly. Confirmed endpoints: /jurisdictions, /people, /people.geo, /bills, /bills/{jurisdiction}/{session}/{bill_id}, /committees, /events, /metrics.

/people.geo: GET params lat (required, number), lng (required, number), include (array, optional extras), apikey/x-api-key. This IS the "find my state legislators by location" endpoint the app needs — confirmed directly from the spec, not just doc prose.

/people (search): filters by jurisdiction, name, id, org_classification (role), district, include, page/per_page. Returns PersonList = {results: [Person], pagination: {per_page, page, max_page, total_items}}. Person fields: id, name, party, current_role {title, org_classification, district, division_id}, jurisdiction, given_name, family_name, image, email, gender, birth_date, other_identifiers, other_names, links, sources, offices.

/bills (search): filters by jurisdiction, session, chamber, identifier, classification, subject, updated_since/created_since/action_since, sort, sponsor, sponsor_classification, q (full text search), include, page/per_page. Returns BillList = {results: [Bill], pagination:{...}}. Bill fields: id, session, jurisdiction, from_organization, identifier, title, classification, subject, first_action_date, latest_action_date, latest_action_description, latest_passage_date, related_bills, abstracts, sponsorships [{name, entity_type, organization, person, primary, classification}], actions [{organization, description, date, classification, related_entities}], sources, versions, documents, votes.

Most useful fields for this app: person.current_role.district/title (map voter's district to their legislator), person.party, person.image, person.offices/links (contact/source links for citations), person.sources (citation URLs); bill.identifier/title/latest_action_description (plain-English status), bill.sponsorships (who to attribute), bill.sources/versions/documents (citation links for "cited insights"), bill.subject (topic tagging).
probes.rate_limits: Not published in the OpenAPI spec, the docs-v3 landing page, or the redirected open.pluralpolicy.com/openstates.org pages — none of these fetchable sources state exact requests/day or requests/minute for the free tier. Community/prior knowledge (unverified today, not to be treated as confirmed) suggests historically ~250 requests/day and ~10 requests/min on the free tier, but I could not confirm this from any page I actually fetched, so treat it as unverified.
probes.app_value: This is the correct backbone for state-level ballot resolution: /people.geo directly answers "who are my state legislators given my lat/lng," and /bills gives sponsor-attributed, sourced legislative activity to build cited voting-record/position insights for state races. Party, district, and current_role fields let the app slot candidates into the right ballot line items.
probes.notes: Live and reachable, but hard-gated by API key for every endpoint (people, people.geo, bills, jurisdictions all returned identical 403 in testing) — there is no keyless/demo tier, so works_now=false until a key is registered. Signup looks low-friction (self-service web signup + profile-page key generation, no evidence of manual review), so registering a key today at hackathon time is realistic. Could not verify actual key-issuance turnaround or rate-limit numbers since that requires an authenticated account action I don't have credentials for. Also could not verify /committees or /events endpoints beyond their existence in the spec (docs note these are experimental/"currently being restored" and data may not be available for all states) — worth a quick smoke test after a key is issued before relying on them.

probes.auth: Free API key required (no working demo/keyless path). Signup: create a free 'OneVote' public account at https://legiscan.com/legiscan, which is also the API key registration form (and shows API status/profile settings once registered). Docs give no stated approval wait, appears self-service, but signup speed itself was NOT verified firsthand (creating an account was out of scope for this probe).
probes.sample_request: Keyless: curl 'https://api.legiscan.com/?op=getSessionList&state=TX' -> HTTP 302 to https://legiscan.com/ (marketing site), which is behind a Cloudflare JS challenge (HTTP 403 'Just a moment...', cf-mitigated: challenge) for non-browser clients -- a bare no-key request never reaches the JSON API. Adding any key param (even garbage) routes into the real JSON API: curl 'https://api.legiscan.com/?key=INVALIDKEY123&op=getSessionList&state=TX' -> HTTP 200, Content-Type application/json, body: {"status":"ERROR","alert":{"message":"Invalid API key format -- <ip> -- /?key=INVALIDKEY123&op=getSessionList&state=TX"}}. Confirms the API is live; only a valid key is missing. Correct shape once a real key exists: https://api.legiscan.com/?key=APIKEY&op=getSessionList&state=TX
probes.response_shape: Pull API (?key=KEY&op=OPERATION&PARAMS), all JSON responses top-level {status:'OK'|'ERROR', ...}. Confirmed via official PDF manual (LegiScan_API_User_Manual.pdf v1.91, fetched and read directly): getSessionList -> {sessions:[{session_id, state_id, year_start, year_end, special, session_name, dataset_hash}]}. getMasterList/getMasterListRaw(id=session_id) -> {masterlist:{'0':{bill_id, number, change_hash, url, status_date, status, last_action, title, description}, ...}}. getBill(id=bill_id) -> {bill:{bill_id, session, state, bill_number, title, description, status, status_date, progress[], history[], sponsors[] (people_id, party, role, name, district, ftm_eid, votesmart_id, opensecrets_id, ballotpedia -- cross-links to campaign-finance/bio sources), subjects[], texts[] (doc_id, url, base64 doc), votes[] (roll_call_id, date, desc, yea/nay/nv/absent/total, passed, url), amendments[], supplements[], calendar[]}}. getRollCall(id=roll_call_id) -> {roll_call:{roll_call_id, bill_id, date, desc, yea/nay/nv/absent/total, passed, votes:[{people_id, vote_id, vote_text:'Yea'|'Nay'|'NV'|'Absent'}]}}. getPerson(id=people_id) -> {person:{people_id, party, role, name, district, ftm_eid, votesmart_id, opensecrets_id, ballotpedia}}. Other ops present: getBillText, getAmendment, getSupplement, getSearch/getSearchRaw (full-text search, 50/2000 results), getDatasetList/getDataset/getDatasetRaw (bulk weekly ZIP snapshots), getSessionPeople, getSponsoredList, getMonitorList/setMonitor.
probes.rate_limits: Confirmed in official manual: "Public service keys have a monthly limit of 30,000 queries to mirror the OneVote public tracking service." A paid Push API tier (real-time webhook updates every 15min-4hr) exists separately but is not needed for a hackathon.
probes.app_value: LegiScan is STATE-LEGISLATURE bill/vote data, not federal candidate/ballot data -- it complements rather than replaces a ballot-resolution source. Its value here: per-legislator voting records on specific bills (getRollCall) to power "how did this incumbent vote" insights, plus getPerson's built-in cross-reference IDs (OpenSecrets, VoteSmart, FollowTheMoney, Ballotpedia) that shortcut linking a state legislator to campaign-finance/bio sources for citations.
probes.notes: Could not test with a real key (none available); getBill/getRollCall/getPerson field lists above are transcribed verbatim from LegiScan's own official PDF manual (authoritative but not independently re-verified against a live authenticated call). Signup turnaround time not verified (account creation was out of scope). The keyless-request behavior is unusual: a bare/no-key request 302-redirects into the human-facing legiscan.com site, which itself sits behind a Cloudflare bot challenge returning 403 to non-browser clients -- only supplying a 'key' param (even invalid) routes into the real JSON API with its clean {status:'ERROR'} format, so don't be fooled by the initial 302/403 into thinking the API is down. Coverage gap: state legislatures only -- no Congress/federal bill coverage in the same way, and NO candidate/election/ballot endpoints at all (no getCandidate, getElection, etc.) -- purely a legislative bill-tracking API, useful only for incumbent voting-record enrichment, not for core ballot/candidate resolution which this app needs from another source (e.g. Open States, Google Civic, or Ballotpedia).

probes.auth: Requires a free Google Cloud API key (enable "Google Civic Information API" in a GCP project, generate key under Credentials). No DEMO_KEY / keyless path exists — every endpoint checks the key before doing anything else. Signup is fast (minutes) via console.cloud.google.com if a teammate has/creates a GCP project, but we could not obtain or test a real key in this probe.
probes.sample_request: curl -i 'https://www.googleapis.com/civicinfo/v2/elections?key=NO_KEY' → HTTP/2 400, body: {"error":{"code":400,"message":"API key not valid. Please pass a valid API key.","status":"INVALID_ARGUMENT", ... "reason":"API_KEY_INVALID"}}. Also tried /representatives?address=...&key=NO_KEY and elections?key=AIzaSyDUMMYKEY123 — both returned the identical 400 API_KEY_INVALID shape. Service itself is alive and responding normally (ESF server, standard Google API error envelope); the 400 is purely an auth gate, not a service outage.
probes.response_shape: voterInfoQuery (GET /civicinfo/v2/voterinfo?address=...&electionId=...&key=...) is the core endpoint for this app. Top-level fields per docs: election{}, otherElections[], normalizedInput{}, contests[], state[], pollingLocations[]/earlyVoteSites[]/dropOffLocations[], mailOnly. Per contest: type, ballotTitle, office, level[], roles[], district{name,scope,id}, numberElected, numberVotingFor, candidates[]{name, party, candidateUrl, phone, email, photoUrl, orderOnBallot, channels[]{type,id} (Facebook/Twitter/YouTube/GooglePlus)}. Referendum-type contests carry referendumTitle/Text/Pro/ConStatement instead of candidates. Location objects carry address, notes, pollingHours, sources[] (with an "official" flag) — useful for citing officialdom. electionQuery (GET /elections) returns a simple {elections:[{id, name, electionDay, ocdDivisionId}]} list used to discover valid electionId values to pass into voterInfoQuery. The representatives resource (representativeInfoByAddress / representativeInfoByDivision) has NO documentation page anymore — https://developers.google.com/civic-information/docs/v2/representatives/representativeInfoByAddress returns a live HTTP 404, and the current v2 reference page (developers.google.com/civic-information/docs/v2) lists only electionQuery, voterInfoQuery, and divisions.search — no representatives entry at all. This is consistent with the reported April 2025 shutdown of the representatives endpoint. There is no explicit "divisionsByAddress replaced representatives" statement in current docs; the surviving /civicinfo/v2/divisions endpoint ("search for political divisions by natural name or OCD ID") is a name/OCD-ID lookup, NOT an address-based rep lookup — it does not return officeholders or officials the way representatives did. Net: post-shutdown, elected-official/representative data (names, offices, contact info, social channels for current officeholders outside of active-election candidates) is NOT retrievable from this API at all; only voterInfoQuery's candidates[] within an active contest gives any person-level political data now.
probes.rate_limits: Docs pages fetched (v2 reference, using_api guide, landing page) contained no explicit quota text — Google no longer documents a fixed daily quota number on these pages (the commonly-cited "25,000 queries/day free" figure is legacy/community-reported and could not be confirmed from current official docs in this probe). Actual quota would need to be checked in the GCP Console "APIs & Services > Quotas" page after enabling the API with a real project — untested here.
probes.app_value: If we get a key, voterInfoQuery is the only remaining piece of this API useful to the voter app: given a home address (+ optional electionId), it returns the voter's actual ballot contests and, for each, the running candidates' names/party/campaign URL/social channels/photo — i.e., authoritative ballot-resolution and candidate-roster data with official-source flags on the polling-location side. It cannot supply incumbent/representative bios, voting records, or campaign-finance links (no such fields exist), and per known behavior it typically returns empty/no contest data until an election is officially loaded into Google's VIP feed, which for Nov 2026 general midterms may not happen until closer to the election (weeks out) — in July 2026 it may only surface already-scheduled state/local primaries, if any, and could well return "no data for this address" for the November general.
probes.notes: Unverified/could not test directly in this probe (no real API key available): (1) actual JSON shape and non-empty contents of a live voterInfoQuery response — only the documented schema was captured via WebFetch, not an executed authenticated call; (2) precise daily quota number; (3) whether Google has already loaded any Nov 2026 midterm or 2026 primary election data into elections/voterInfoQuery as of July 2026 — would need a valid key to call GET /elections and inspect the returned list live. Confirmed via live HTTP 404 that the representatives endpoint's documentation page no longer exists, and confirmed via curl that the API gateway is alive and uniformly gates all endpoints on key validity (elections, representatives, and a dummy-formatted key all produced the identical API_KEY_INVALID 400). Recommendation for hackathon: do NOT rely on Civic Information API for representative/officeholder bios or campaign finance (that data source is gone); only use it, if at all and only after confirming a key works, as a possible ballot/candidate-roster lookup for whichever races Google has already loaded, with a fallback (e.g., Ballotpedia, state SOS data, or manual seed data) for anything it returns empty.

probes.auth: None required. All requests returned HTTP 200 with no Authorization header sent and no API key/token in the query string. No rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After) were present in responses, so throttling behavior/limits with vs. without a key could not be observed empirically — official docs (lda.senate.gov/api) mention registering for a free token for write access and higher limits, but read (GET) endpoints worked anonymously in all 3 test calls with no degradation or 429s observed in this short probe.
probes.response_shape: Top-level: {"count": int, "next": url|null, "previous": url|null, "results": [filing, ...]}. Each filing object includes: filing_uuid, filing_type/filing_type_display (e.g. "RR"=Registration), filing_year, filing_period_display (e.g. "1st Quarter"), filing_document_url (link to the actual PDF/HTML filing — good citation source), income, expenses, expenses_method_display, dt_posted, termination_date, registrant_state; nested registrant{name, address, contact_name/telephone} (the lobbying firm); nested client{name, general_description, state, ppb_state, ppb_country} (who is paying for the lobbying); and lobbying_activities[] array each with general_issue_code_display (e.g. "Budget/Appropriations"), description (free-text issue specifics, e.g. "FY26 defense appropriations and authorizations"), and a nested lobbyists[] array with lobbyist{first_name,last_name} per activity. Note: this filings endpoint does NOT include a direct "which member of Congress" field — issue descriptions are free text; there is no structured link from a filing to a specific Senator/Representative. Cross-referencing to "your incumbent" would require text-matching legislator names/bill numbers inside the free-text issue descriptions, or using LDA's separate constants/bill lookups if available.
probes.rate_limits: No rate-limit headers observed in 3 successful anonymous GET requests (no X-RateLimit-*, no 429s hit). Per Senate LDA docs (not independently verified live here), unauthenticated/registered users can read filings freely at low-to-moderate volume; write/search operations and higher-volume programmatic use are said to benefit from a free API token via senate.gov account signup. For a hackathon's read-only "who lobbies your incumbent" feature, anonymous access appears entirely sufficient based on this probe.
probes.app_value: Gives Tier 2 "who's lobbying your incumbent" content: for a client/company or issue-area search you get the lobbying firm (registrant), the paying client, the specific lobbyist names, the general issue category, a free-text description of the specific issue, and a direct link to the official filing document to cite as a source — all without needing any API key, which is ideal for a time-boxed hackathon build.
probes.notes: Response headers included deprecation/sunset notices ("sunset: Fri, 31 Jul 2026 23:59:59 GMT", pointing to a successor API at lda.gov/api/v1/) — worth noting this exact endpoint is slated for retirement after the target Nov 2026 election window opens, though it should remain live through the hackathon and election. Could not verify: (1) actual numeric rate limits or any distinction between keyed/unkeyed throughput since no throttling was triggered or headers exposed in this small probe; (2) whether a registered API token unlocks additional filters or endpoints, since I did not sign up for one; (3) any direct filing-to-legislator linkage — filings link to registrants/clients/lobbyists but not to specific members of Congress, so matching "your incumbent" would require additional text-matching logic on issue descriptions or supplementing with another source (e.g. a bill-sponsor lookup) to connect lobbying activity to a specific Senator/Representative.

probes.auth: none — public GitHub repo, no key needed. GitHub REST API (api.github.com) is rate-limited to 60 req/hour per IP when unauthenticated; raw.githubusercontent.com (used for actual CSV fetches) is a CDN with no meaningful per-IP limit for hackathon-scale use.
probes.sample_request: curl 'https://api.github.com/repos/openelections/openelections-data-tx/contents/2024/counties' -> 200 OK, lists files like 20241105__tx__general__anderson__precinct.csv with a download_url pointing to raw.githubusercontent.com. Then: curl 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/openelections/openelections-data-tx/master/2024/counties/20241105__tx__general__anderson__precinct.csv' -> 200 OK, plain CSV text.
probes.response_shape: GitHub contents API: JSON array of objects with name, path, sha, size, url, download_url, html_url, type ('file'/'dir'). Raw CSV (observed header for TX 2024 general, county-precinct level): county,precinct,office,district,party,candidate,votes,absentee,early_voting,election_day. Sample rows include office='President'/'U.S. Senate'/'U.S. House' (district=6 populated for House races)/'Railroad Commissioner', party=REP/DEM/LIB/GRN, candidate full name, votes as integer, plus vote-method breakdowns (absentee/early_voting/election_day). Rows also include non-candidate rows like 'Registered Voters' and 'Ballots Cast' mixed into the same office column.
probes.rate_limits: api.github.com: 60 requests/hour unauthenticated (per source IP) — fine for browsing directory listings a few times, but don't loop it per-voter at runtime. raw.githubusercontent.com: no documented per-IP cap for this volume, effectively unlimited for hackathon use; best practice is to fetch/cache CSVs once at build time or via a periodic refresh, not on every user request.
probes.app_value: Gives real, verified historical precinct/county-level vote totals per office/district/party for Texas's 2024 general election (and presumably prior cycles under other year folders in the same repo) — exactly the raw material needed to compute 'this district went 60/40 last time' for U.S. House and other district races, since the district column is populated for districted offices.
probes.notes: Verified only openelections-data-tx (Texas) since that's the repo the task named; other states live in sibling repos (openelections-data-<state>), not checked here, and coverage/format consistency across states is NOT verified — OpenElections is a volunteer-maintained project and historically has uneven completeness/format by state and year (some states/years use statewide files instead of per-county precinct files; some report only county-level totals with no precinct breakdown). No 2026 data exists yet (project only publishes results after elections occur; today is 2026-07-03, before the Nov 2026 midterms), so for the target election this is strictly a HISTORICAL baseline source ('last time' results), not a source for the current ballot/candidates — matches the intended use case. The office column is a free-text label mixing turnout metadata rows (e.g. 'Registered Voters', 'Ballots Cast') with actual contests, so a parser must filter to rows matching known contest names. District field is present but empty for statewide/at-large offices (President, Senate, Railroad Commissioner) and populated only for districted races (U.S. House=6 in the sample) — parser needs a fallback lookup for statewide races. Only inspected one county file (Anderson, TX) and one state within the 8-request budget; did not verify state legislative (TX House/Senate) rows or other states/years.

probes.auth: No key needed for search/metadata (fully open, worked immediately). But actual file downloads are gated by a mandatory Harvard Dataverse "Guestbook" response (guestbookID 458, applies dataverse-wide to MEDSL's collections) — this is a data-usage terms/contact-info form, not an API key, and there is no anonymous/keyless bypass found via query params (tried default, ?format=original, ?gbrecs=true, and Range header — all returned the same 400 guestbook error).
probes.sample_request:
1) curl 'https://dataverse.harvard.edu/api/search?q=medsl+county+presidential&type=dataset&per_page=3' → HTTP 200, found "County Presidential Election Returns 2000-2024" doi:10.7910/DVN/VOQCHQ.
2) curl 'https://dataverse.harvard.edu/api/datasets/:persistentId/?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VOQCHQ' → HTTP 200, full dataset metadata + file list (file IDs, filenames, sizes).
3) curl -L 'https://dataverse.harvard.edu/api/access/datafile/13573089?format=original' → HTTP 400: {"status":"ERROR","message":"You may not download this file without the required Guestbook response for guestbookID 458."} (same error reproduced on the markdown codebook file 11723285, and on the U.S. House 1976-2024 dataset doi:10.7910/DVN/IG0UN2, file 13592823 — confirms it's a collection-wide policy, not a one-off file setting).
probes.response_shape: Search API: {"status":"OK","data":{"total_count","items":[{"name","type","url","global_id"(DOI),"description","publisher","citation","identifier_of_dataverse","fileCount","versionState","authors",...}]}}. Dataset metadata API: {"data":{"latestVersion":{"guestbookId","license","metadataBlocks":{"citation":{...title/author/description/subject...}},"files":[{"dataFile":{"id","filename","contentType","filesize","...}}]}}}. Most useful fields for the app: global_id (DOI, stable dataset ref), file dataFile.id (needed for /api/access/datafile/{id}), filename, contentType, filesize, dsDescription, publicationDate/updatedAt (vintage), license (CC0 — good for reuse), and the dataset's guestbookId (tells you up front if download will be gated).
probes.rate_limits: No rate-limit headers observed in these ~6 requests; Harvard Dataverse is a shared public instance and imposes general fair-use limits but nothing hit here. No key required for the endpoints that did work.
probes.app_value: Would be a strong historical/backfill source for county-level presidential and U.S. House returns (2000-2024, CC0-licensed, well-documented, MIT-maintained) — but only for showing past election context/turnout patterns, not for resolving a live 2026 ballot (no candidate bios, positions, or finance data, and it's historical returns, not real-time/future races).
probes.notes: Findable programmatically: yes, cleanly, via the keyless Dataverse search + dataset-metadata APIs (JSON, well-structured, DOIs stable). Downloadable programmatically: NO for anonymous/no-account access — every file tested triggers a mandatory Guestbook response requirement (this is Harvard Dataverse's own IRB/terms-acknowledgment gate, separate from any MEDSL API key concept; MEDSL has no API key system at all here, it's pure Dataverse). To actually pull the .tab/.csv bytes you need either: (a) a Harvard Dataverse account + API token and to submit the guestbook form once via the web UI (or account-authenticated API call) per dataset, then reuse the token for subsequent downloads, or (b) manually download through the browser (fills guestbook form → gets a session cookie/link) and vend the file another way (e.g., re-host on GitHub/S3 for the hackathon). I did not have credentials to test the authenticated-token path, so I cannot confirm whether an API token alone (without a completed guestbook submission) suffices — Dataverse docs suggest the guestbook is generally enforced per-user regardless of token, but I could not verify this live. Practical hackathon verdict: treat as "manual one-time download, then self-host the CSV" rather than "live API pull" — it is NOT a zero-friction bulk-CSV-via-API source despite having no cost/API-key barrier.

mcp_eval.reasoning:
Use a plain ingest→normalize→store (SQLite/JSON) pipeline with ordinary function calls as the app's runtime data-access layer for the hackathon. Do NOT build the 6-tool MCP server as the thing the deployed app talks to at runtime — under the confirmed current stack (OpenRouter) it buys zero functional benefit over plain functions, adds a process/transport/session layer, and is invisible to judges in a live demo. It IS worth spinning up cheaply as a Claude-Code-facing dev convenience (query live civic data while building normalizers) since FastMCP is trivially thin and Claude Code speaks MCP natively already. Revisit MCP for the *runtime* path only if the team commits post-hackathon (or as a stretch goal) to the Anthropic Messages API, whose mcp_servers/mcp_toolset connector natively executes MCP tool calls server-side — a real capability OpenRouter's API and @openrouter/agent do not have.

Detailed findings:
1) Effort to stand up the 6-tool MCP server: Low. PyPI 'mcp' package (v1.28.1, maintained by Anthropic/PBC, MIT, "Production/Stable") ships FastMCP with a decorator API — a tool is literally `@mcp.tool()` over a plain Python function. Writing 6 thin wrappers around functions that must exist anyway (get_ballot, get_candidate, get_finance, get_votes, search_bills, get_district) is ~2-4 hours if the underlying query/normalize functions already exist, since the MCP layer adds only signature/docstring/transport boilerplate, not new logic. Add another 2-4 hours if you also need to expose it over public HTTPS/SSE (required for Anthropic's connector or for team members outside your machine) rather than local stdio.

2) @openrouter/agent v0.7.2 does NOT support MCP servers as tool providers. Fetched npm registry metadata (registry.npmjs.org/@openrouter/agent/latest): description is "Agent toolkit... tool orchestration, streaming, multi-turn conversations, and format compatibility"; deps are only zod + @openrouter/sdk. Fetched the actual package README (raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenRouterTeam/typescript-agent/main/packages/agent/README.md) and grepped case-insensitively for "mcp" — 0 matches. Its "format compatibility" exports (openai-compat, anthropic-compat, claude-constants) are about reconciling OpenAI vs Anthropic chat-completions shapes, not MCP.

3) OpenRouter's chat-completions API has no native MCP parameter — confirmed via WebFetch of OpenRouter's own cookbook page (openrouter.ai/docs/cookbook/coding-agents/mcp-servers): it explicitly shows a client-side pattern — converting MCP tool defs to OpenAI-compatible tool schemas via your own code, using Anthropic's MCP client SDK to hold the stateful session, then sending a normal chat-completion request. The doc's own words: "interacting with MCP servers is more complex than calling a REST endpoint. The MCP protocol is stateful and requires session management." In contrast, Anthropic's Messages API MCP connector (fetched platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/mcp-connector, after a redirect from docs.claude.com) has genuine native support: `mcp_servers` array (type "url", must be https, optional OAuth authorization_token) + `mcp_toolset` entries in `tools`, requires header `anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20`, returns `mcp_tool_use`/`mcp_tool_result` content blocks, and Anthropic's backend itself makes the outbound MCP call. Limitations: only remote HTTP/SSE servers (no local stdio), not ZDR-eligible, allowlist/denylist per tool. Live-probed api.anthropic.com/v1/messages with a `mcp_servers` field and no API key/beta header: got HTTP 401 `{"type":"authentication_error","message":"x-api-key header is required"}` — i.e. it got past body parsing to the auth check rather than rejecting the field outright, consistent with the field being a recognized top-level param (not proof the beta header requirement was satisfied, since no valid key was available to test that far — noted as unverified beyond auth-error stage).

4) Dev-time value is real and cheap: Claude Code (the environment building this app) consumes MCP servers natively via .mcp.json. A ~2-4 hour FastMCP wrapper around your civic-data fetchers, added to the dev environment's MCP config, lets you (the builder) ask Claude Code to pull live ballot/candidate/FEC/vote data conversationally while writing ingestion code and prompts — decoupled from whatever the shipped app uses at runtime. This is the one place MCP clearly pays for itself at hackathon speed.

5) Demo/judge value vs cost: MCP plumbing is invisible in a live demo — judges see ballot lookups, sourced insights, and UI, not the transport protocol behind your data layer. The wow factor of "we used MCP" is negligible against a general-voter audience and even against most hackathon judges unless they specifically care about agentic architecture. Meanwhile the time cost (schema definitions, transport/auth choice, keeping a second process alive, debugging session handshakes) directly competes with time needed for ballot-data correctness, citation UI, and personalization quality — the actual judged criteria. Net: skip it as the runtime layer now; keep it in your back pocket as a fast follow-on if you settle on Anthropic API post-hackathon.
mcp_eval.effort_hours: Runtime MCP server (6 tools) as app data layer: ~4-8 hrs (2-4 hrs of thin FastMCP wrapping over functions you must write anyway, +2-4 hrs if exposed over public HTTPS for remote/Anthropic-connector use). Dev-time-only local FastMCP server for Claude Code: ~2-4 hrs. Plain SQLite/JSON pipeline + ordinary function calls: no separate estimate needed — it's the functions you're writing regardless; MCP is pure incremental overhead on top of it, not a replacement for it.
mcp_eval.runtime_support: OpenRouter (today's provider) has no native MCP consumption at the chat-completions API level, and @openrouter/agent v0.7.2 (confirmed via its npm-published README, 0 mentions of "mcp") does not bridge MCP either — OpenRouter's own cookbook doc says MCP integration must be done client-side (convert MCP tools → OpenAI tool schema yourself, manage the stateful MCP session yourself). So under OpenRouter, "wrap it in MCP" saves nothing over calling functions directly, since you still hand-write the tool-schema/execution glue either way. Anthropic's Messages API is the only stack member with real native MCP consumption: the `mcp_servers` + `mcp_toolset` params (beta header `mcp-client-2025-11-20`, confirmed live via platform.claude.com/docs and a probe request) let Anthropic's backend itself call out to a public HTTPS/SSE MCP server and return `mcp_tool_use`/`mcp_tool_result` blocks — but only if the team switches the app's runtime LLM provider to Anthropic and hosts the MCP server publicly (local stdio is not supported by the connector).
mcp_eval.dev_time_value: Genuinely worthwhile and cheap: Claude Code (the dev tool being used to build this very app) natively consumes MCP servers via .mcp.json. A ~2-4 hour FastMCP server wrapping the same civic-data fetch functions lets the team query live ballot/candidate/FEC/vote-record data conversationally through Claude Code while writing normalizers, schemas, and prompts — independent of whatever the shipped app uses at runtime. This is the strongest justification for touching MCP at all during the hackathon, decoupled entirely from the runtime-architecture decision.
mcp_eval.alternatives: Plain ingest→normalize→store (SQLite/JSON) pipeline with ordinary Python function calls (get_ballot(), get_candidate(), get_finance(), get_votes(), search_bills(), get_district() as normal functions/DB queries invoked directly by the backend, wrapped into OpenAI-style tool-call schemas only where an LLM call needs them) delivers identical capability with far less code: no transport layer, no client/server handshake or session state, no extra process to keep alive during a live demo, and it works unchanged regardless of which LLM provider ends up serving the app (OpenRouter now, possibly Anthropic later). This is the recommended hackathon-runtime path; MCP is additive tooling for dev-time convenience only, and a future migration path if the provider decision lands on Anthropic.
evidence: https://registry.npmjs.org/@openrouter/agent (npm registry metadata: description, deps zod+@openrouter/sdk, homepage) — fetched via curl, HTTP 200
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenRouterTeam/typescript-agent/main/README.md — monorepo README, fetched via curl, HTTP 200, no MCP mention
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/OpenRouterTeam/typescript-agent/main/packages/agent/README.md — fetched via curl, grep -ic mcp = 0 (zero mentions of MCP)
https://pypi.org/pypi/mcp/json — fetched via curl, HTTP 200, version 1.28.1, author Anthropic PBC, includes FastMCP quickstart showing decorator-based @mcp.tool() API
https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/mcp-servers — curl returned client-rendered Next.js shell (not useful directly, HTTP 401 JSON on unrelated chat-completions probe)
https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions — curl POST with no key, HTTP 401 JSON auth error observed, confirms endpoint is OpenAI-style chat completions with no MCP param in play

https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages — curl POST including an mcp_servers field, no API key, HTTP 401 {"type":"authentication_error","message":"x-api-key header is required"} (parsed body before failing on auth, no beta header sent so beta-gating unverified)

https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/mcp-connector (redirected from docs.claude.com) — WebFetch, full MCP connector docs: mcp_servers/mcp_toolset shape, anthropic-beta: mcp-client-2025-11-20 header, remote HTTP/SSE only, mcp_tool_use/mcp_tool_result response blocks, not ZDR-eligible

https://openrouter.ai/docs/cookbook/coding-agents/mcp-servers — WebFetch, confirms MCP-with-OpenRouter is a client-side conversion pattern (stateful session management, tool-schema conversion), not a native API parameter

WebSearch "OpenRouter API MCP servers support chat completions mcp parameter" — corroborated no native OpenRouter MCP param; multiple third-party MCP-server wrappers exist around OpenRouter but none are OpenRouter's own API adding native MCP support
